title: Plans for Reducing the Size of the Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet
summary: The Federal Reserve announced plans to reduce its balance sheet starting June 1, 2022, by setting caps on reinvestment of principal payments received from securities held in the System Open Market Account (SOMA). Treasury securities caps will start at $30 billion per month and increase to $60 billion per month after three months, while agency debt and mortgage-backed securities caps will start at $17.5 billion per month and increase to $35 billion per month after three months.
key_points: QT to begin June 1, 2022 with caps on reinvestment of principal payments
Treasury securities monthly cap: $30bn initially, rising to $60bn after three months
Agency debt and agency MBS monthly cap: $17.5bn initially, rising to $35bn after three months
Treasury coupon securities and bills will decline under the monthly cap
The Committee intends to maintain securities holdings in amounts needed for an 'ample reserves regime'
Runoff will slow and stop when reserve balances are 'somewhat above' the level judged ample
why_it_matters: This announcement formalized the Fed's Quantitative Tightening (QT) program, marking a significant shift from the pandemic-era QE. The pace of balance sheet reduction—up to $95bn per month combined cap—represented a substantial tightening of financial conditions and affected Treasury and MBS markets directly.
macro_context: This QT announcement accompanied the May 2022 FOMC meeting where the Fed also raised rates by 50 basis points. The dual tightening via rate hikes and balance sheet reduction signaled aggressive policy normalization to combat inflation. The source does not provide forward-looking market impact predictions.
market_structure_context: The announcement details the operational mechanics of balance sheet runoff, specifically the monthly caps on SOMA reinvestment. It describes the transition to an 'ample reserves regime' and notes that once runoff ceases, reserve balances will continue declining until they reach an 'ample level'.
notes: Historical record. This is an official Federal Reserve press release from May 2022 announcing the start of Quantitative Tightening. The content is factual and well-sourced but predates TODAY by nearly 4 years. MARKET_IMPACT marked as unknown/mixed confidence because the source does not discuss market impact directly; the historical impact is outside knowledge not present in source text.
